headline: Timber Industry Revenue Falls 22% as Deforestation Regulations Bite
context: Malaysia's timber export revenue fell 22% to RM18.4 billion in 2025 as the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) excluded Malaysian timber products from key European markets pending compliance certification that 62% of exporters failed to obtain.

cards.big: RM5.2 billion in timber exports lost to EU deforestation rules — 62% of Malaysian exporters can't prove legal sourcing
cards.sub: EUDR requires plot-level traceability to 2020 baseline — Malaysia's timber tracking system doesn't go below concession level

cards.big: Timber sector employs 380,000 workers and contributes 1.1% of GDP — EU market loss threatens 85,000 jobs directly
cards.sub: Indonesia invested RM1.2 billion in EUDR compliance systems since 2023 — Malaysia allocated only RM180 million

cards.big: Indigenous communities managing 2.4 million hectares of community forests cannot access EUDR certification due to customary land title issues
cards.sub: Smallholders producing 28% of timber exports excluded from certification benefits designed for plantation-scale operations

cards.big: MTIB and Forestry Department operate separate timber tracking databases that don't interoperate
cards.sub: Compliance certification requires data from 6 agencies — no single authority can issue the end-to-end chain of custody document EUDR demands

cards.big: Malaysia had 4 years' notice of EUDR — losing RM5.2 billion because compliance preparation wasn't done isn't a trade barrier, it's a governance failure
Should Malaysia create a unified timber traceability platform combining all 6 agencies into one EUDR-compliant system within 12 months?
